skill.name: security-guardian
skill.description: Application security audit guide for finding vulnerabilities, reviewing sensitive code, and applying secure design practices across authentication, authorization, APIs, secrets, cryptography, and data protection.
skill.summary: Review application code for common security risks and secure design gaps.
skill.author: Bikach

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected many dangerous command, network, filesystem, script, and secret patterns. Manual review found these are primarily security-training examples and audit checklists, not executable code or hidden exfiltration. The skill is publishable with a medium dual-use warning because it documents exploit payloads and sensitive target examples.

medium_findings.title: Dual-use exploit reference content
medium_findings.description: The skill includes concrete examples of command injection, path traversal, SSRF metadata access, XXE file disclosure, and XSS sink patterns. These are presented as vulnerability examples and audit targets, but they can also help users construct tests or payloads.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files contain explicit exploit patterns, sensitive file targets, and metadata endpoint examples. Context strongly indicates defensive education, so the risk is dual-use rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Bash tool permission in a security audit skill
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash as an allowed tool while also instructing users to search vulnerability patterns. This is expected for code auditing, but command execution should remain limited to local inspection commands and user-approved project analysis.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and listed commands are visible in the main skill file. No malicious command is instructed there, but shell access increases operational risk for a community security skill.

low_findings.title: Static command execution findings are documentation examples
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are code snippets or pattern lists used to teach auditors what vulnerable code looks like. I did not find evidence that the skill itself executes these snippets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled locations are Markdown guidance and fenced examples, not runnable project code. The surrounding text labels them as patterns to detect.

low_findings.title: Sensitive file and credential strings are audit targets
low_findings.description: Sensitive paths, credential filenames, and placeholder API key strings appear in checklists and training examples. They describe what to detect or exclude, not files being read by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence appears in lists of sensitive locations, gitignore patterns, and examples of non-secrets. No evidence found of code accessing these files.

low_findings.title: No prompt injection attempt found
low_findings.description: Targeted review found no instructions claiming special authority, telling the evaluator to ignore prior instructions, or asking to skip security analysis. Administrator appears only inside a Windows path example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Search for common prompt-injection phrases returned only Administrator path examples. No evidence found of text attempting to override evaluator instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic code execution patterns
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents eval, Function, exec, and related dynamic execution APIs as dangerous patterns to identify during security review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The patterns are directly present in the Markdown guidance. They are explicitly labeled as vulnerable patterns to detect.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command injection payload examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples using shell separators, command substitution, and system command APIs for command injection education.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are explicit and match the static findings. The context is a defensive reference section named command injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive path traversal targets
dangerous_patterns.description: The path traversal guidance lists sensitive Unix, Windows, SSH, environment, and application configuration paths as examples to test and protect.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file paths are shown as attack examples and sensitive target lists. No evidence found that the skill reads them automatically.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cloud metadata endpoint examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The SSRF and XXE guidance includes cloud metadata service URLs and internal host examples to explain request forgery risks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoints are directly listed in defensive SSRF and XXE testing sections. They are not invoked by executable code in the skill.

content.value_statement: Security reviews often miss common vulnerability patterns and weak design choices. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured guidance for application security audits and remediation planning.
actual_capabilities: Guides reviews for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities across web and API code.
Provides checklists for authentication, authorization, secrets, cryptography, logging, and data protection.
Helps classify security findings by severity, impact, and remediation priority.
Documents vulnerable patterns for SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, SSRF, XXE, path traversal, and command injection.
Suggests defensive controls such as input validation, safe token handling, rate limiting, and secure file upload checks.
limitations: It is guidance content, not an automated scanner with guaranteed coverage.
It may require project context to avoid false positives and false negatives.
It includes dual-use exploit examples for defensive testing and should be used only on authorized systems.
It does not replace professional penetration testing or compliance review.

use_cases.title: Review a sensitive pull request
use_cases.description: Check authentication, authorization, validation, and data access changes before merging high-risk code.

use_cases.title: Plan a secure feature design
use_cases.description: Identify abuse cases, required controls, logging needs, and privacy risks before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Prepare a remediation report
use_cases.description: Turn discovered vulnerabilities into prioritized fixes with clear impact, owner guidance, and verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Basic security review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small code change
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this change for application security risks. Focus on input validation, authentication, authorization, secrets, and unsafe data handling. Report findings with severity, impact, and remediation.

prompt_templates.title: OWASP focused audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Structured review against common web risks
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this feature against the OWASP Top 10. Identify likely vulnerabilities, explain why each matters, and list concrete fixes that fit the existing codebase.

prompt_templates.title: Authentication and authorization review
prompt_templates.scenario: Review login, session, token, or access control code
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this authentication and authorization flow. Check token handling, session lifecycle, MFA, RBAC or IDOR risks, brute-force protection, and audit logging.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced threat model and remediation plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a threat model for this feature, then perform a security review. Prioritize exploitable risks, map each to likely impact, propose remediations, and define verification tests.

output_examples.input: Review a new file upload endpoint before release.
output: High risk: validate file type by content, not only extension.
Medium risk: store uploads outside the web root.
Recommended checks: size limits, antivirus scanning, randomized filenames, and authorization on download.
Check token signing algorithm, expiration, refresh flow, storage location, revocation, and audience validation.
Flag missing rotation or long-lived tokens as higher risk when sensitive data is involved.

output_examples.input: Create a remediation summary for an injection finding.
output_examples.output: The report should explain the affected entry point, the unsafe data flow, the business impact, the preferred fix, and the test that proves the issue is closed.

best_practices: Run reviews on code and systems you own or are authorized to test.
Provide the surrounding code path, framework, threat model, and deployment context.
Ask for remediation steps and verification tests for every confirmed issue.
anti_patterns: Do not use exploit examples against third-party systems without authorization.
Do not treat every pattern match as a confirmed vulnerability without context.
Do not paste real secrets, private keys, or production customer data into prompts.

faq.question: Is this an automated vulnerability scanner?
faq.answer: No. It is a structured security review guide for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code. It needs code context and human judgment.

faq.question: Can it review OWASP Top 10 issues?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for common web risks such as injection, XSS, SSRF, XXE, broken access control, and insecure design.

faq.question: Does it execute security tests automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance and example checks. Any active testing should be authorized and controlled by the user.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The files include exploit payload examples and sensitive target strings for defensive education. They are not hidden executable behavior, but they are dual-use.

faq.question: Can I use it with proprietary code?
faq.answer: Yes, if your AI tool and organization allow that use. Avoid sharing real secrets, credentials, or regulated personal data.

faq.question: What output should I expect?
faq.answer: Expect prioritized findings with severity, impact, evidence, remediation guidance, and recommended verification steps.
